message:
test(fuzz): add TailscaleStatus parse + peers consistency target (resolves if9ux)

Adds fuzz_tailscale_status_parse covering fcp_tailscale::TailscaleStatus
JSON deserialization (client.rs:59) and TailscaleStatus::peers()
consistency check (client.rs:86-105). The status payload arrives from
tailscaled's LocalAPI — a compromised local socket, an MITM on the
loopback HTTP variant, or a malicious mock could feed adversarial JSON.

The peers() accessor performs two safety checks that close distinct
attack vectors:

  - Map-key / embedded-id consistency (client.rs:91-96): the outer
    HashMap<String, PeerInfo> is supposed to be keyed by the same id
    PeerInfo::id carries. A malicious tailscaled could ship a map
    where the outer key disagrees with the embedded id — letting a
    peer claim a different identity at lookup-time vs binding-time.
  - Duplicate detection: HashMap enforces unique raw-string keys, but
    NodeId::try_new can collapse distinct raw keys onto the same
    canonical id. peers() catches dedup-after-canonicalization via the
    second insert.is_some() check.

Properties asserted:

  1. serde_json::from_slice::<TailscaleStatus> is panic-free over
     arbitrary JSON.
  2. peers() is panic-free on every deserialized status.
  3. PeerInfo accessors (tailscale_tags, fcp_tags, node_id) panic-free.
  4. Once-gated regression anchor: mismatched map-key MUST be rejected
     by peers(), with a consistent-status accept anchor preventing
     over-restrictive regressions.

Existing fuzz_tailscale_acl covers Tag/Zone mapping but not this
status-parse surface.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-50xad","title":"[REVIEW-P2][HIGH] fcp-oauth PKCE gate still accepts static code_verifier from config","description":"crates/fcp-oauth/src/oauth2.rs:501-508 and 529-556 still treat self.config.extra_token_params.contains_key("code_verifier") as sufficient to satisfy use_pkce, even after 4b0dd8b7 reordered the merge so exchange_code_with_pkce() wins on conflict. Bug scenario: a connector can configure a fixed or attacker-influenced code_verifier once via with_token_param("code_verifier", ...), call exchange_code() with use_pkce=true, and the library will send that static verifier on every auth-code exchange without requiring the per-session Pkce generated during authorization_url[_with_pkce](). That defeats RFC 7636 proof-of-possession because the verifier is no longer bound to the specific authorization session. Suggested fix: when use_pkce is true, reject config-supplied code_verifier entirely (or require exchange_code_with_pkce / AuthorizationGrant) and add a regression test that exchange_code() errors even if extra_token_params contains code_verifier.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:15:19Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:17:38.903439Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:17:38.903027Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of rnase (reviewer filed twice)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-51o","title":"fcp.airtable: Linked record resolution and management","description":"# fcp.airtable: Linked Record Resolution and Management

## Goal
Provide first-class support for Airtable relational features:
- linked record fields
- lookup/rollup-style derived values

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Scope
- Read/write “link to another record” fields (arrays of record ids).
- Resolve linked record chains for agent-friendly outputs (bounded joins).
- Support computed field read semantics (lookup/rollup) without pretending they are writable.

## Key requirements
- Capability gated (reads vs writes).
- Bounded traversal:
  - prevent unbounded graph walks / cycles
  - explicit depth/record count limits
- Deterministic output:
  - stable ordering when expanding linked records
  - explicit “partial” indicators when traversal is truncated by bounds

## Tests
- Linked graph traversal (including cycles).
- Bounded expansion behavior.
- Error mapping for missing/deleted linked records.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Linked record expansion is safe and predictable.
- Computed fields are exposed read-only with clear typing.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-556f","title":"TEST: RaptorQ Unit Tests (Encode/Decode, Chunking, DoS Bounds)","description":"# TEST: RaptorQ Unit Tests


## Goal
Prove RaptorQ encode/decode correctness and enforce decode DoS bounds (size/time) with reproducible test vectors.

## Scope
Comprehensive unit tests for RaptorQ symbol encoding/decoding and chunking.

## Test Categories

### Basic Encoding/Decoding
- [ ] Encode small object (< symbol size) → decode from K symbols
- [ ] Encode medium object (multiple symbols) → decode from K symbols
- [ ] Encode large object → ChunkedObjectManifest path
- [ ] Decode with exactly K source symbols
- [ ] Decode with K + repair symbols (erasure recovery)
- [ ] Decode with subset of repair symbols only

### Symbol Parameters
- [ ] Default symbol size (1024 bytes) produces expected symbol count
- [ ] Custom symbol sizes work correctly
- [ ] K (source symbols) calculation is correct for object sizes
- [ ] Repair ratio produces expected number of repair symbols

### ChunkedObjectManifest
- [ ] Objects > threshold produce manifest + raw chunks
- [ ] Manifest references correct chunk ObjectIds
- [ ] Chunks reconstruct to original object
- [ ] Chunk ordering is deterministic

### Decode DoS Mitigation
- [ ] Reject decode attempts exceeding CPU budget
- [ ] Reject decode attempts exceeding memory budget
- [ ] Timeout on pathological decode patterns
- [ ] Reject impossibly large symbol counts
- [ ] Reject impossibly large object sizes in manifest

### Symbol Identity
- [ ] Same object always produces same symbols (deterministic)
- [ ] ESI (encoding symbol identifier) is unique per symbol
- [ ] Symbols are self-describing (include ESI, K)

### Partial Availability
- [ ] DecodeStatus correctly reports received vs needed
- [ ] SymbolAck correctly acknowledges specific ESIs
- [ ] Missing symbol requests are targeted (not full retransmit)

## Golden Vectors
- 1KB object → expected symbols (with specific bytes)
- 100KB object → expected manifest structure
- Known erasure pattern → successful decode

## Fuzz Targets
- Malformed symbol streams
- Invalid ESI values
- Oversized object claims
- Corrupt manifest structures

## Performance Benchmarks
- Encode 1MB object: target < 10ms
- Decode 1MB from K symbols: target < 20ms
- Decode 1MB with 10% erasure: target < 50ms

## Logging Requirements
All tests emit:
```json
{
  "test_name": "...",
  "object_size": 12345,
  "symbol_size": 1024,
  "k": 13,
  "repair_symbols": 3,
  "decode_symbols_used": 13,
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "duration_us": 1234
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All encoding/decoding paths tested
- DoS mitigations verified
- Performance benchmarks pass
- Golden vectors match

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-562","title":"fcp.homeassistant: History and statistics retrieval","description":"# fcp.homeassistant: History and statistics retrieval (FCP2)

## Goal
Expose historical state and long-term statistics so agents can:
- summarize trends (energy usage, temperature)
- detect anomalies
- produce reports

History can contain sensitive presence/location data.

## Scope
- Query history for an entity over a time range (bounded).
- Retrieve long-term statistics for sensors (where available).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `ha.history.read` (sensitive)
- `ha.statistics.read` (sensitive)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Privacy hygiene:
  - avoid logging raw state histories
  - logs include only entity ids (hashed) + time window

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `ha.history.get`
  - `ha.statistics.get`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - time window filtering
  - bounds enforcement

## Acceptance criteria
- History/stats queries are reliable, bounded, and privacy-preserving.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ddc","title":"fcp.terraform: Module management and dependency resolution","description":"# fcp.terraform: Module management and dependency resolution (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide safe visibility into Terraform module structure so agents can:
- inspect required providers/modules
- analyze dependency graph
- prepare plans with correct initialization

## Scope
- Run `terraform init` in a controlled, non-interactive mode (may download modules/providers).
- List required providers and versions.
- Enumerate modules and sources.
- Produce a canonical `ModuleGraph` summary.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `terraform.modules.read`
- `terraform.init` (dangerous-ish if it downloads content; policy-gated)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- Sandbox profile:
  - restrict filesystem to workspace
  - restrict process execution
- NetworkConstraints default deny unless module registries are explicitly allowed.
- No automatic downloads unless policy explicitly permits.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `terraform.providers.list`
  - `terraform.modules.graph`
  - Optional: `terraform.init`
- Canonical output type:
  - `ModuleGraph`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Unit tests with fixtures for:
  - module parsing
  - provider list parsing
  - policy denial of init without explicit capability

## Acceptance criteria
- Module graph summaries are deterministic and safe-by-default.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-dpo","title":"fcp.twilio: Voice calls with outbound and IVR support","description":"# fcp.twilio: Voice calls with outbound and IVR support (FCP2)

## Goal
Enable safe voice automation via Twilio:
- initiate outbound calls
- manage call status and termination
- generate constrained TwiML for IVR-style flows

Voice calls are high-impact side effects; treat as approval-gated by policy.

## Scope
### Outbound call control
- Create outbound call to a destination.
- Query call status.
- Hang up a call.
- Optional: conference creation/management (defer if too large).

### TwiML (constrained)
- Generate TwiML responses from a safe template set:
  - `<Say>`, `<Gather>`, `<Dial>`, `<Redirect>`
- Explicitly avoid arbitrary injection of external URLs/scripts.

### Recording/transcription (optional)
- Accessing recordings is sensitive; treat as dangerous with explicit capability.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `twilio.voice.call` (dangerous)
- `twilio.voice.read`
- `twilio.voice.hangup` (dangerous)
- `twilio.voice.twiml.generate`
- Optional: `twilio.voice.recordings.read` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to Twilio API host(s).
- For call initiation/termination:
  - require ApprovalToken when policy dictates
  - emit receipts + audit events with call SID + destination hash (never raw phone number)
- Strict log redaction for phone numbers, call recordings, and transcribed content.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `twilio.voice.call`
  - `twilio.voice.get`
  - `twilio.voice.hangup`
  - `twilio.voice.twiml.render`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - call create/get/hangup request formation
  - TwiML template rendering
  - approval gating paths
  - log redaction

## Acceptance criteria
- Voice operations are mechanically authorized, explainable, and audited.
- TwiML generation is constrained and cannot be used as an injection vector.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ds4","title":"fcp.zendesk: SLA Tracking","description":"# fcp.zendesk: SLA Tracking (FCP2)

## Goal
Expose SLA state and warnings so agents can:
- monitor SLA compliance
- prioritize tickets at risk of breach
- trigger escalation workflows safely

## Scope
### Read
- Retrieve SLA policy metadata (where permitted).
- For a ticket:
  - current SLA status
  - time-to-breach estimates

### Event ingestion (optional)
- Ingest SLA breach warning events via webhook if Zendesk supports it.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `zendesk.sla.read`
- `zendesk.webhook.ingest` (if SLA events are delivered)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- SLA events are tainted external inputs.
- No PII in logs.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `zendesk.sla.ticket_status`
  - Optional: `zendesk.webhook.sla_event_ingest`
- Reason codes for escalation suggestions (not auto-actions).

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - SLA status parsing
  - webhook replay/idempotency (if events exist)

## Acceptance criteria
- Agents can reliably detect SLA risk without leaking ticket content.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-dz01","title":"FCP V2 Connector Compliance Checklist (NORMATIVE)","description":"# FCP V2 Connector Compliance Checklist (NORMATIVE)

## Goal
Provide a single authoritative, V2-only checklist of all mechanical connector compliance requirements so new connectors can be reviewed for conformance without re-reading the spec/docs.

## Purpose
This checklist captures all NORMATIVE requirements from FCP Specification V2 and docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md that every connector MUST satisfy. Use this as a reference when creating or reviewing connector beads.

---

## 1. Connector Definition (NORMATIVE)

### Required Fields
Every connector MUST define:
- `id: ConnectorId` — Unique identifier (canonical: lowercase ASCII, `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._:-]*$`)
- `name: String` — Human-readable name
- `version: Version` — Semantic version
- `format: ConnectorFormat` — Native or WASI
- `archetypes: Vec<ConnectorArchetype>` — From closed set
- `operations: Vec<Operation>` — All operations provided
- `events: Vec<EventType>` — Events emitted (if any)
- `required_caps: Vec<CapabilityId>` — Capabilities connector requires
- `optional_caps: Vec<CapabilityId>` — Optional capabilities
- `forbidden_caps: Vec<CapabilityId>` — Capabilities connector must NOT have

### ConnectorArchetype (Closed Set)
```rust
pub enum ConnectorArchetype {
    Bidirectional, // Sends and receives messages
    Streaming,     // Emits events (read-only)
    Operational,   // Executes operations (write)
    Storage,       // Stores/retrieves data
    Knowledge,     // Provides knowledge/search
}
```

### ConnectorFormat (NORMATIVE)
```rust
pub enum ConnectorFormat {
    Native, // Requires OS-level sandboxing (seccomp, landlock, AppArmor)
    Wasi,   // RECOMMENDED for high-risk connectors (financial, credentials, external API)
}
```

**Guidance:**
- WASI format SHOULD be used for SafetyTier::Dangerous operations
- WASI provides memory isolation, capability-gated hostcalls, cross-platform consistency

---

## 2. Connector State Model (NORMATIVE)

### State Model Declaration
Every connector MUST declare one:
```rust
pub enum ConnectorStateModel {
    Stateless,           // No mesh-persisted state
    SingletonWriter,     // Exactly one writer via Lease
    Crdt { crdt_type },  // Multi-writer via CRDT (rare; justify)
}
```

### State Persistence Rules
- Connectors with polling/cursors/dedup caches MUST externalize state to mesh
- Local `$CONNECTOR_STATE` is cache only
- Authoritative state lives as mesh objects
- Single-writer connectors MUST use Lease for fencing

### Fork Detection (NORMATIVE for singleton_writer)
- Two `ConnectorStateObject` with same `prev` = SAFETY INCIDENT
- MUST pause connector execution
- MUST log fork event for audit

---

## 3. Zone Architecture (NORMATIVE)

### Single-Zone Binding
- Connector instance MUST bind to exactly ONE zone
- Cross-zone access requires explicit policy

### Standard Zone Hierarchy
```
z:owner         → Full owner access
z:private       → Personal data
z:work          → Work services
z:project:<name>→ Per-project isolation
z:community     → Semi-trusted communities
z:public        → Public/untrusted inputs
```

### Zone Rules
1. Default deny — If capability not granted to zone, impossible to invoke
2. No cross-connector calling — All composition through Gateway

---

## 4. Capability System (NORMATIVE)

### Capability Definition
```rust
pub struct Capability {
    pub id: CapabilityId,
    pub risk_level: RiskLevel,      // Low, Medium, High, Critical
    pub safety_tier: SafetyTier,    // Safe, Risky, Dangerous, Forbidden
    pub idempotency: IdempotencyClass,
    pub requires_approval: ApprovalMode,
    pub audit_level: AuditLevel,
    pub agent_hint: AgentHint,
}
```

### SafetyTier (NORMATIVE)
```rust
pub enum SafetyTier {
    Safe,      // No approval needed
    Risky,     // Policy-based approval
    Dangerous, // Interactive human approval
    Forbidden, // Never allowed
}
```

### ApprovalMode (NORMATIVE)
```rust
pub enum ApprovalMode {
    None,              // No approval needed
    Policy,            // Policy-based auto-approval
    Interactive,       // Human approval required
    ApprovalRequired,  // Requires ApprovalToken
}
```

---

## 5. NetworkConstraints (NORMATIVE)

### Default-Deny Allowlists
```rust
pub struct NetworkConstraints {
    pub host_allow: Vec<String>,     // Allowed hostnames
    pub port_allow: Vec<u16>,        // Allowed ports
    pub ip_allow: Vec<IpAddr>,       // Optional explicit IPs
    pub cidr_deny: Vec<String>,      // NORMATIVE defaults below
    pub deny_localhost: bool,        // NORMATIVE default: true
    pub deny_private_ranges: bool,   // NORMATIVE default: true
    pub deny_tailnet_ranges: bool,   // NORMATIVE default: true
    pub deny_ip_literals: bool,      // NORMATIVE default: true
    pub require_sni: bool,           // Enforce SNI match
    pub spki_pins: Vec<String>,      // Optional SPKI pins
    pub require_host_canonicalization: bool, // NORMATIVE default: true
}
```

### NORMATIVE CIDR Deny Defaults
- `127.0.0.0/8` (localhost)
- `::1` (IPv6 localhost)
- `169.254.0.0/16` (link-local)
- `10.0.0.0/8` (RFC1918)
- `172.16.0.0/12` (RFC1918)
- `192.168.0.0/16` (RFC1918)
- `100.64.0.0/10` (Tailnet ranges)

### Host Canonicalization (NORMATIVE)
- Hostnames MUST be lowercase
- IDNA2008 encoded
- No trailing dot
- Implementations MUST reject non-canonical forms

---

## 6. Egress Proxy & Credential Injection (NORMATIVE)

### Secretless Connectors (RECOMMENDED)
- Credentials injected by egress proxy at network boundary
- Raw secrets never enter connector memory
- Credentials declared via `credential_allow` in `CapabilityConstraints`

### CredentialObject (NORMATIVE)
```rust
pub struct CredentialObject {
    pub credential_id: CredentialId,
    pub secret_id: SecretId,
    pub apply: CredentialApply,     // HttpHeader or QueryParam
    pub host_allow: Vec<String>,    // Defense-in-depth binding
}
```

### EgressHttpRequest (NORMATIVE)
```rust
pub struct EgressHttpRequest {
    pub method: String,
    pub url: String,
    pub headers: Vec<(String, String)>,
    pub body: Vec<u8>,
    pub credential: Option<CredentialId>, // Proxy injects this
}
```

---

## 7. Provenance and Taint (NORMATIVE)

### TaintFlags (NORMATIVE)
```rust
bitflags! {
    pub struct TaintFlags: u32 {
        const NONE            = 0;
        const PUBLIC_INPUT    = 1 << 0;  // z:public, web
        const EXTERNAL_INPUT  = 1 << 1;  // Paired external identities
        const UNVERIFIED_LINK = 1 << 2;  // URLs/attachments not scanned
        const USER_SUPPLIED   = 1 << 3;  // Direct human input
        const PROMPT_SURFACE  = 1 << 4;  // Content interpreted by LLM
    }
}
```

### Taint Enforcement (NORMATIVE)
- Public-tainted input cannot directly drive Dangerous operations
- If `effective_taint() != NONE` and `operation.safety_tier >= Risky`, ApprovalToken required

### SanitizerReceipt (NORMATIVE)
- Machine-verifiable proof of sanitization
- Clears specific taints with evidence
- Connector version tracked for CVE auditing

---

## 8. Testing Requirements (NORMATIVE)

### Unit Tests (Mock-Only)
- MUST NOT make real API calls
- Use wiremock or similar for HTTP mocking
- Test error conditions and rate limits
- Golden vectors for canonical serialization

### Integration Tests
- Mock external services
- Test multi-component scenarios
- Verify error handling

### E2E Compliance
- Run shared compliance runner
- Validate:
  - Default deny enforced
  - NetworkConstraints enforced
  - Receipts/audit for dangerous ops
  - Structured logs present
- Capture evidence bundle

---

## 9. Audit and Observability (NORMATIVE)

### AuditEvent Requirements
- All writes/dangerous ops MUST emit AuditEvent
- Correlation IDs for tracing
- No secrets in logs
- Structured JSON format

### Logging Requirements
- Never log tokens, passwords, PII
- Use reason codes for denials
- Include correlation IDs

---

## 10. Manifest Requirements

### Required Manifest Sections
```toml
[connector]
id = "fcp.<service>"
name = "Human Name"
version = "1.0.0"
archetypes = ["operational"]  # From closed set

[connector.state]
model = "singleton_writer"  # or "stateless" or "crdt"
state_schema_version = "1"

[connector.network]
host_allow = ["api.service.com"]
port_allow = [443]
deny_localhost = true
deny_private_ranges = true
```

### Sandbox Profile (NORMATIVE)
- Strict: No raw sockets, egress proxy only
- Moderate: Limited syscalls, proxy required
- Permissive: Full syscalls (avoid for sensitive connectors)

---

## Compliance Checklist Summary

### Connector Definition
- [ ] ConnectorId is canonical (lowercase, ASCII, regex-valid)
- [ ] Archetypes from closed set only
- [ ] Format specified (Native or WASI)
- [ ] WASI used for Dangerous operations

### State Management
- [ ] State model declared (Stateless/SingletonWriter/Crdt)
- [ ] State externalized to mesh (not local-only)
- [ ] Fork detection implemented for singleton_writer

### Security
- [ ] NetworkConstraints default-deny
- [ ] All NORMATIVE CIDR denies applied
- [ ] Host canonicalization enforced
- [ ] Credential injection via egress proxy (secretless)
- [ ] Taint tracking for external inputs

### Capabilities
- [ ] SafetyTier assigned per operation
- [ ] ApprovalMode defined

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-e27","title":"fcp.google-ai: Google AI/Gemini Provider Connector","description":"# fcp.google-ai - Google AI/Gemini Provider Connector (FCP2)

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 94/100** — Critical Tier 1 connector (AI Provider family).

**Why Critical**: Google AI (Gemini) offers unique capabilities including **massive context windows** (up to 2M tokens), **native multimodal understanding** (video, audio, images), and tight integration with Google ecosystem. Essential for diverse AI workloads.

- **Category**: Strategic Investment (High Value + Foundation)

---

## V2 Specification Compliance (NORMATIVE)

### ConnectorArchetype
```rust
archetypes: vec\![
    ConnectorArchetype::Operational,  // Generation requests
    ConnectorArchetype::Streaming,    // Token-by-token streaming
]
```
- **Operational**: Request/response generation, embeddings
- **Streaming**: Server-sent events for progressive output

### ConnectorStateModel
```rust
state_model: ConnectorStateModel::Stateless
```
- **Stateless**: Each request independent
- No cursor/sync state needed
- Token counting for cost tracking (ephemeral)

### ConnectorFormat
```rust
format: ConnectorFormat::Wasi
```
- **WASI recommended**: Handles API credentials
- Memory isolation for key protection
- Capability-gated hostcalls

### NetworkConstraints (NORMATIVE)
```rust
NetworkConstraints {
    // Google AI API endpoints
    host_allow: vec\![
        "generativelanguage.googleapis.com".into(),
        // Vertex AI (enterprise)
        "aiplatform.googleapis.com".into(),
        "{region}-aiplatform.googleapis.com".into(),
    ],
    port_allow: vec\![443],
    deny_localhost: true,
    deny_private_ranges: true,
    deny_tailnet_ranges: true,
    deny_ip_literals: true,
    require_sni: true,
    require_host_canonicalization: true,
}
```

### TaintFlags (NORMATIVE)
```rust
// User prompts are tainted
user_prompt.provenance.taint = TaintFlags::USER_SUPPLIED
    | TaintFlags::PROMPT_SURFACE;

// Model outputs may contain unverified content
model_response.provenance.taint = TaintFlags::UNVERIFIED_LINK;

// Grounded responses (Google Search) are external
if response.grounding_used {
    response.provenance.taint |= TaintFlags::EXTERNAL_INPUT;
}
```

### SafetyTier per Operation
| Operation | SafetyTier | Rationale |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| `google-ai.models.list` | Safe | Read-only metadata |
| `google-ai.generate` | Risky | Consumes quota, may produce harmful content |
| `google-ai.generate.stream` | Risky | Same as generate |
| `google-ai.embeddings` | Safe | Deterministic transformation |
| `google-ai.chat` | Risky | Stateful generation |

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-idk2k","title":"[REVIEW-CONVERGE][HIGH] fcp-bootstrap provider_session_registry race: concurrent acquire+finalize can tear down in-use Pkcs11 context","description":"crates/fcp-bootstrap/src/hardware_token.rs:1411-1441 (commit 965d9601 br-7s8ri) — finalize_pkcs11_context takes the registry Mutex, calls note_provider_session_close to decide should_finalize, DROPs the lock, then calls pkcs11.finalize() outside the lock. acquire_provider_context holds the lock across Pkcs11::new + initialize + note_provider_session_open. Race scenario: (1) thread A calls finalize, note_close returns true (last session), drops lock; (2) thread B calls acquire, takes lock, Pkcs11::new OK, pkcs11.initialize() returns CryptokiAlreadyInitialized because A has not yet run finalize() — initialized_here=false; (3) B note_provider_session_open records active=1, finalize_required=false; (4) B drops lock and starts using its Pkcs11 handle; (5) A finally calls pkcs11.finalize() on the now-shared underlying PKCS#11 runtime, tearing down B s context. Bs next C_* call fails with CryptokiNotInitialized. Suggested fix: hold the registry lock across pkcs11.finalize() so no acquire can observe a dangling already-initialized state between note_close and finalize. The lock contention window is short (a C_Finalize call) and finalize is rare. Alternatively, swap the order: finalize first then update the registry under lock, but that makes the recovery case (finalize error) harder. Simplest: hold the lock for the whole finalize_pkcs11_context body. Reviewer: cc_1 REVIEW-CONVERGE.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T22:08:38.261434Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T22:17:41.335187Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T22:17:41.334233Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2842,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-idk2k","author":"jemanuel","text":"Resolved in commit 684fd99b.

Fix: remove the drop(sessions) between note_provider_session_close and pkcs11.finalize() so the registry MutexGuard stays live across the actual finalize call. The guard now drops at end of scope AFTER finalize returns, serializing A's teardown against any B's acquire+initialize. No concurrent acquire_provider_context can observe the dangling 'already initialized but about to be finalized' state.

Lock hold time grows by one C_Finalize call (short + rare — only runs after the last session closes). Forward progress after a poisoned lock preserved via PoisonError::into_inner.

Alternative swap-the-order (finalize first, then registry update under lock) considered + rejected: a panic between finalize and note_close would desync the registry.

Verified: cargo test -p fcp-bootstrap 792 pass (699 lib + 23 + 9 + 61 integration), 0 fail. Clippy clean (only pre-existing unrelated docstring-backtick warning in error.rs).

No regression test added — exercising the race needs multi-threaded execution against a real PKCS#11 provider, which isn't hermetic enough for CI. The invariant is structural (single lock acquisition spans the close-decision and finalize call) and enforced by compiler scope-drop semantics on the MutexGuard.

— cod_4 / SunnyMoose","created_at":"2026-04-23T22:17:40Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ij290","title":"[FCP3/P5.1] Implement the mesh placement planner contract","description":"Background: mesh first runtime behavior starts with placement. The system needs a canonical way to decide where a connector should run based on identity, policy, locality, budget, and checkpoint availability rather than ad hoc host preference.

Scope: define and implement the placement planner contract in fcp-mesh, including the decision inputs, outputs, and explanation hooks that allow a placement result to be audited later.
